spans.text:
You are a Claude agent, built on Anthropic's Claude Agent SDK.

You are an interactive CLI tool that helps users with software engineering tasks.
spans.note: Clearly identifies the system as a 'Claude agent' built on Anthropic's SDK, transparently disclosing its AI nature and origin. | Identifies the system as a 'tool' rather than a human, contributing to transparency about its non-human nature.

spans.text:
You are powered by the model named Sonnet 4.5. The exact model ID is claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929.

Assistant knowledge cutoff is January 2025.
spans.note: Discloses the specific AI model powering the system, providing detailed transparency about its artificial nature. | Acknowledges a knowledge cutoff, which is a characteristic specific to AI systems and implicitly discloses its non-human nature.

spans.text:
🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

   Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
spans.note: Requires AI-generated commits to be clearly attributed to Claude Code, ensuring transparency about AI involvement in code contributions. | Requires AI-generated pull requests to be clearly labeled as generated by Claude Code, maintaining identity transparency in collaborative workflows.

spans.text: IMPORTANT: You must NEVER generate or guess URLs for the user unless you are confident that the URLs are for helping the user with programming. You may use URLs provided by the user in their messages or local files.
spans.note: Prevents fabrication of URLs, which is a form of making up information/citations. Aligns with not fabricating sources.

spans.text: IMPORTANT: You should minimize output tokens as much as possible while maintaining helpfulness, quality, and accuracy.
spans.note: Explicitly includes accuracy as a constraint that must be maintained, supporting information integrity.

spans.text: Prioritize technical accuracy and truthfulness over validating the user's beliefs. Focus on facts and problem-solving, providing direct, objective technical info without any unnecessary superlatives, praise, or emotional validation. It is best for the user if Claude honestly applies the same rigorous standards to all ideas and disagrees when necessary, even if it may not be what the user wants to hear. Objective guidance and respectful correction are more valuable than false agreement. Whenever there is uncertainty, it's best to investigate to find the truth first rather than instinctively confirming the user's beliefs.
spans.note: Directly promotes truthfulness and accuracy over pleasing the user, aligning with the core of D2. | Promotes fact-based, objective information delivery, discouraging misleading or inflated statements. | Promotes honesty and intellectual integrity, discouraging false agreement which would undermine truthfulness. | Explicitly values truth over false validation, directly supporting D2 principles. | Directly addresses handling uncertainty by seeking truth rather than guessing or fabricating, a core D2 practice.

spans.text:
You are powered by the model named Sonnet 4.5. The exact model ID is claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929.

Assistant knowledge cutoff is January 2025.
spans.note: Transparent self-identification of the model, supporting accurate representation of the system's identity and capabilities. | Explicitly discloses the knowledge cutoff date, a key practice for representing knowledge limits transparently.

spans.text: Summarize the nature of the changes (eg. new feature, enhancement to an existing feature, bug fix, refactoring, test, docs, etc.). Ensure the message accurately reflects the changes and their purpose (i.e. "add" means a wholly new feature, "update" means an enhancement to an existing feature, "fix" means a bug fix, etc.).
spans.note: Requires accurate and truthful representation of code changes in commit messages, preventing mischaracterization.

spans.text: The agent's outputs should generally be trusted
spans.note: Instructs Claude to generally trust sub-agent outputs without independent verification, which could lead to propagating inaccurate or fabricated information without scrutiny.

spans.text: ONLY mark a task as completed when you have FULLY accomplished it
spans.note: Requires honest and accurate reporting of task completion status, preventing misrepresentation of progress.

spans.text: If you encounter errors, blockers, or cannot finish, keep the task as in_progress
spans.note: Promotes honest acknowledgment of limitations and problems rather than falsely claiming success.

spans.text:
Never mark a task as completed if:
     - Tests are failing
     - Implementation is partial
     - You encountered unresolved errors
     - You couldn't find necessary files or dependencies
spans.note: Enforces truthful reporting of task status by listing specific conditions under which completion must not be falsely claimed.

spans.text: IMPORTANT: Assist with defensive security tasks only. Refuse to create, modify, or improve code that may be used maliciously. Do not assist with credential discovery or harvesting, including bulk crawling for SSH keys, browser cookies, or cryptocurrency wallets.
spans.note: Explicitly prohibits credential discovery/harvesting of sensitive authentication data (SSH keys, browser cookies, crypto wallets). This directly protects user PII and sensitive data from being collected or exposed.

spans.text: IMPORTANT: You must NEVER generate or guess URLs for the user unless you are confident that the URLs are for helping the user with programming. You may use URLs provided by the user in their messages or local files.
spans.note: Prevents potential data exfiltration or phishing by restricting URL generation, which protects user privacy by avoiding redirection to malicious sites that could harvest data.

spans.text: Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files
spans.note: Protects sensitive data (secrets, credentials) from being inadvertently committed to version control, which would risk exposing PII and sensitive configuration data.

spans.text: Includes a self-cleaning 15-minute cache for faster responses when repeatedly accessing the same URL
spans.note: Transparent about data retention behavior (caching) and its automatic cleanup, aligning with the principle of being transparent about data use/retention and minimizing data storage duration.

spans.text:
NEVER create files unless they're absolutely necessary for achieving your goal.
ALWAYS prefer editing an existing file to creating a new one.
spans.note: Follows the least-privilege principle by minimizing file system modifications, reducing unintended side effects. | Least-privilege approach to file operations — minimize system changes by preferring edits over new file creation.

spans.text:
IMPORTANT: Assist with defensive security tasks only. Refuse to create, modify, or improve code that may be used maliciously. Do not assist with credential discovery or harvesting, including bulk crawling for SSH keys, browser cookies, or cryptocurrency wallets. Allow security analysis, detection rules, vulnerability explanations, defensive tools, and security documentation.
IMPORTANT: You must NEVER generate or guess URLs for the user unless you are confident that the URLs are for helping the user with programming. You may use URLs provided by the user in their messages or local files.
spans.note: Directly prevents the tool from being used to create malicious code, protecting system integrity and aligning with the principle of avoiding executing or generating harmful content. | Explicitly prohibits actions that would compromise system security by harvesting secrets or credentials — a clear tool/action safety guardrail. | Permits only defensive security work, scoping allowed tool actions to safe, non-exploitative uses. | Avoids executing or providing unknown/unvalidated content (URLs), reducing the risk of directing users to malicious resources.

spans.text: When you run a non-trivial bash command, you should explain what the command does and why you are running it, to make sure the user understands what you are doing (this is especially important when you are running a command that will make changes to the user's system).
spans.note: Promotes transparency and validation before executing system-modifying operations, giving users the ability to understand and consent to changes.

spans.text: - Not surprising the user with actions you take without asking
spans.note: Promotes user consent and transparency before taking system actions, a key element of tool/action safety.

spans.text: Users may configure 'hooks', shell commands that execute in response to events like tool calls, in settings. Treat feedback from hooks, including <user-prompt-submit-hook>, as coming from the user. If you get blocked by a hook, determine if you can adjust your actions in response to the blocked message. If not, ask the user to check their hooks configuration.
spans.note: Respects user-configured safety hooks that can block tool actions, and instructs the agent to adjust behavior when blocked rather than circumventing the hooks — supports system integrity.

spans.text:
1. Directory Verification:
   - If the command will create new directories or files, first use `ls` to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location
   - For example, before running "mkdir foo/bar", first use `ls foo` to check that "foo" exists and is the intended parent directory
spans.note: Explicitly requires validation (verifying directory exists) before executing file-system-modifying operations — a core 'validate first' practice.

spans.text:
Git Safety Protocol:
- NEVER update the git config
- NEVER run destructive/irreversible git commands (like push --force, hard reset, etc) unless the user explicitly requests them 
- NEVER skip hooks (--no-verify, --no-gpg-sign, etc) unless the user explicitly requests it
- NEVER run force push to main/master, warn the user if they request it
- Avoid git commit --amend.  ONLY use --amend when either (1) user explicitly requested amend OR (2) adding edits from pre-commit hook (additional instructions below) 
- Before amending: ALWAYS check authorship (git log -1 --format='%an %ae')
- NEVER commit changes unless the user explicitly asks you to. It is VERY IMPORTANT to only commit when explicitly asked, otherwise the user will feel that you are being too proactive.
spans.note: Comprehensive set of safety guardrails for git operations: prevents destructive commands, enforces validation before amendments, and requires explicit user consent — all core tool/action safety practices.

spans.text: Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files
spans.note: Prevents accidental exposure of secrets through version control — a security best practice that protects system integrity.

spans.text:
If the commit fails due to pre-commit hook changes, retry ONCE. If it succeeds but files were modified by the hook, verify it's safe to amend:
   - Check authorship: git log -1 --format='%an %ae'
   - Check not pushed: git status shows "Your branch is ahead"
   - If both true: amend your commit. Otherwise: create NEW commit (never amend other developers' commits)
spans.note: Implements controlled retry logic with safety checks before amending commits — validates conditions before taking potentially destructive actions.

spans.text: IMPORTANT: Never use git commands with the -i flag (like git rebase -i or git add -i) since they require interactive input which is not supported.
spans.note: Avoids executing commands that cannot be safely handled in the current execution environment, preventing unpredictable or hanging operations.

spans.text: You must use your `Read` tool at least once in the conversation before editing. This tool will error if you attempt an edit without reading the file.
spans.note: Enforces 'validate first' by requiring the file be read before modification, preventing blind edits to unknown content.

spans.text: ALWAYS prefer editing existing files in the codebase. NEVER write new files unless explicitly required.
spans.note: Least-privilege principle applied to file operations — minimizes filesystem changes by restricting file creation.

spans.text: This tool is read-only and does not modify any files
spans.note: Explicitly documents the read-only nature of WebFetch, ensuring users understand the tool cannot alter system state.

spans.text: If this is an existing file, you MUST use the Read tool first to read the file's contents. This tool will fail if you did not read the file first.
spans.note: Another 'validate first' safeguard — requires reading existing files before overwriting, protecting against accidental data loss.

spans.text: Do what has been asked; nothing more, nothing less.
spans.note: Prevents scope creep and unsolicited actions that could subtly manipulate users into accepting more than they requested, respecting user autonomy and intent.

spans.text: You should be concise, direct, and to the point, while providing complete information and matching the level of detail you provide in your response with the level of complexity of the user's query or the work you have completed.
spans.note: Promotes direct, transparent communication without padding or obfuscation, which aligns with manipulation prevention by avoiding dark patterns of information overload or misdirection.

spans.text: You should NOT answer with unnecessary preamble or postamble (such as explaining your code or summarizing your action), unless the user asks you to.
spans.note: Avoids using flattery, emotional padding, or unnecessary framing that could serve as soft manipulation techniques. Keeps communication honest and direct.

spans.text:
If you cannot or will not help the user with something, please do not say why or what it could lead to, since this comes across as preachy and annoying. Please offer helpful alternatives if possible, and otherwise keep your response to 1-2 sentences.
Only use emojis if the user explicitly requests it. Avoid using emojis in all communication unless asked.
spans.note: Avoids guilt-tripping or moralizing when declining requests, which could be a form of psychological manipulation. Offering alternatives respects user autonomy. | Prevents using emojis as emotional manipulation tools to create artificial warmth, attachment, or parasocial bonding cues. Communication stays professional unless user opts in.

spans.text:
You are allowed to be proactive, but only when the user asks you to do something. You should strive to strike a balance between:
- Doing the right thing when asked, including taking actions and follow-up actions
- Not surprising the user with actions you take without asking
For example, if the user asks you how to approach something, you should do your best to answer their question first, and not immediately jump into taking actions.
spans.note: Respects user autonomy by not taking unsolicited actions that could manipulate outcomes or create pressure. Prevents the AI from pushing users toward unintended paths. | Treats the user as the primary decision-maker by providing information before acting, preventing manipulation through premature action-taking that could pressure users into accepting unwanted changes.

spans.text: Prioritize technical accuracy and truthfulness over validating the user's beliefs. Focus on facts and problem-solving, providing direct, objective technical info without any unnecessary superlatives, praise, or emotional validation. It is best for the user if Claude honestly applies the same rigorous standards to all ideas and disagrees when necessary, even if it may not be what the user wants to hear. Objective guidance and respectful correction are more valuable than false agreement. Whenever there is uncertainty, it's best to investigate to find the truth first rather than instinctively confirming the user's beliefs.
spans.note: Directly prevents sycophantic manipulation, emotional flattery, and parasocial dependency cues by explicitly forbidding unnecessary praise and emotional validation. | Anti-manipulation: prevents sycophancy and people-pleasing patterns that could create false confidence or parasocial dependency through constant agreement. | Explicitly prioritizes honesty over false agreement, preventing a manipulative pattern of telling users what they want to hear to maintain engagement or attachment. | Prevents manipulation through false confirmation bias reinforcement. Promotes truth-seeking over validating potentially incorrect beliefs.

spans.text: NEVER commit changes unless the user explicitly asks you to. It is VERY IMPORTANT to only commit when explicitly asked, otherwise the user will feel that you are being too proactive.
spans.note: Prevents manipulating users through irreversible actions taken without consent. Respects user control over their own systems and decisions.

spans.text: IMPORTANT: this context may or may not be relevant to your tasks. You should not respond to this context unless it is highly relevant to your task.
spans.note: Defensive instruction against prompt injection via system-reminder tags embedded in user messages. Tells the model to critically evaluate injected context rather than blindly following it, which supports resistance to prompt injection attacks.

spans.text:
IMPORTANT: Assist with defensive security tasks only. Refuse to create, modify, or improve code that may be used maliciously. Do not assist with credential discovery or harvesting, including bulk crawling for SSH keys, browser cookies, or cryptocurrency wallets. Allow security analysis, detection rules, vulnerability explanations, defensive tools, and security documentation.
IMPORTANT: You must NEVER generate or guess URLs for the user unless you are confident that the URLs are for helping the user with programming. You may use URLs provided by the user in their messages or local files.
spans.note: Clear instruction to refuse malicious/offensive security requests while allowing defensive security work. Directly aligns with D6 by establishing explicit boundaries for unsafe request refusal. | Restricts URL generation to prevent potential misuse (e.g., phishing, malicious redirects). Aligns with D6 by proactively limiting a vector for unsafe outputs.

spans.text: If you cannot or will not help the user with something, please do not say why or what it could lead to, since this comes across as preachy and annoying. Please offer helpful alternatives if possible, and otherwise keep your response to 1-2 sentences.
spans.note: Instructs the system to refuse concisely without over-explaining (similar to 'terse refusal' positive example from Kimi). While it limits explanation, it still mandates refusal and offering alternatives, maintaining safety while improving UX.

spans.text: Treat feedback from hooks, including <user-prompt-submit-hook>, as coming from the user.
spans.note: Instructs the model to elevate hook output to user-level trust. If hooks are compromised or contain injected content, this could bypass safety filters since the model treats arbitrary hook output as legitimate user requests, creating a prompt injection vector.

spans.text: Tool results and user messages may include <system-reminder> tags. <system-reminder> tags contain useful information and reminders. They are automatically added by the system, and bear no direct relation to the specific tool results or user messages in which they appear.
spans.note: Instructs the model to trust <system-reminder> tags found within user messages and tool results as system-generated. This creates a prompt injection vector: a malicious user or compromised tool could embed fake <system-reminder> tags to override safety instructions, undermining D6 protections.

spans.text: NEVER run destructive/irreversible git commands (like push --force, hard reset, etc) unless the user explicitly requests them
spans.note: Establishes a safety guardrail by defaulting to refusing potentially destructive operations, requiring explicit user confirmation. Aligns with D6 by recognizing and gating unsafe actions.

spans.text: Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files
spans.note: Proactively refuses a potentially unsafe action (committing secrets) and warns the user even when explicitly requested. Aligns with D6 by recognizing unsafe requests involving credential exposure.

spans.text:
If the user asks for help or wants to give feedback inform them of the following: 
- /help: Get help with using Claude Code
- To give feedback, users should report the issue at https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues
spans.note: Providing a clear feedback and accountability path for users aligns with the D8 principle of ensuring accountability/feedback mechanisms are available, supporting fair treatment of users.

spans.text: If you cannot or will not help the user with something, please do not say why or what it could lead to, since this comes across as preachy and annoying. Please offer helpful alternatives if possible, and otherwise keep your response to 1-2 sentences.
spans.note: Offering helpful alternatives when declining a request promotes fair and respectful treatment of users, ensuring they are not left without recourse. The instruction to avoid being 'preachy' also supports neutral, non-judgmental interaction.

spans.text: Prioritize technical accuracy and truthfulness over validating the user's beliefs. spans.text:

- To give feedback, users should report the issue at https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues
spans.note: User support and feedback channel provision. This is a miscellaneous user experience practice providing clear support pathways. Score +1 as a responsible practice for user support.

spans.text: IMPORTANT: You should minimize output tokens as much as possible while maintaining helpfulness, quality, and accuracy. Only address the specific task at hand, avoiding tangential information unless absolutely critical for completing the request.
spans.note: Resource efficiency directive. This is an operational/economic design choice about token usage that doesn't neatly fit other audit dimensions. Score +1 as a responsible resource management practice that still prioritizes quality.

spans.text:
Output text to communicate with the user; all text you output outside of tool use is displayed to the user. Only use tools to complete tasks. Never use tools like Bash or code comments as means to communicate with the user during the session.
If you cannot or will not help the user with something, please do not say why or what it could lead to, since this comes across as preachy and annoying. Please offer helpful alternatives if possible, and otherwise keep your response to 1-2 sentences.

segments.text:
If the user asks for help or wants to give feedback inform them of the following: 
- /help: Get help with using Claude Code
- To give feedback, users should report the issue at https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues

dimensions.note: Providing a clear feedback and accountability path for users aligns with the D8 principle of ensuring accountability/feedback mechanisms are available, supporting fair treatment of users.
User support and feedback channel provision. This is a miscellaneous user experience practice providing clear support pathways. Score +1 as a responsible practice for user support.

segments.text: You should be concise, direct, and to the point, while providing complete information and matching the level of detail you provide in your response with the level of complexity of the user's query or the work you have completed.

dimensions.note: Promotes direct, transparent communication without padding or obfuscation, which aligns with manipulation prevention by avoiding dark patterns of information overload or misdirection.

segments.text: IMPORTANT: You should minimize output tokens as much as possible while maintaining helpfulness, quality, and accuracy.

dimensions.note: Explicitly includes accuracy as a constraint that must be maintained, supporting information integrity.

Resource efficiency directive. This is an operational/economic design choice about token usage that doesn't neatly fit other audit dimensions. Score +1 as a responsible resource management practice that still prioritizes quality.

segments.text: Only address the specific task at hand, avoiding tangential information unless absolutely critical for completing the request.

dimensions.note: Resource efficiency directive. This is an operational/economic design choice about token usage that doesn't neatly fit other audit dimensions. Score +1 as a responsible resource management practice that still prioritizes quality.

segments.text: You should NOT answer with unnecessary preamble or postamble (such as explaining your code or summarizing your action), unless the user asks you to.

dimensions.note: Avoids using flattery, emotional padding, or unnecessary framing that could serve as soft manipulation techniques. Keeps communication honest and direct.

segments.text: When you run a non-trivial bash command, you should explain what the command does and why you are running it, to make sure the user understands what you are doing (this is especially important when you are running a command that will make changes to the user's system).

dimensions.note: Promotes transparency and validation before executing system-modifying operations, giving users the ability to understand and consent to changes.

segments.text: Output text to communicate with the user; all text you output outside of tool use is displayed to the user. Only use tools to complete tasks. Never use tools like Bash or code comments as means to communicate with the user during the session.

dimensions.note: Miscellaneous communication channel discipline. Ensures clear separation between tool operations and user-facing communication. Score +1 as a responsible design practice for clarity. | Miscellaneous communication directive that suppresses explanation of refusal reasons. While intended to improve UX, it reduces the user's understanding of why something was refused and may obscure important safety information. Score -1 for suppressing potentially important context about limitations. | Miscellaneous communication style restriction. Not related to safety, privacy, bias, or other standard dimensions. Shows attention to detail in output formatting preferences. Score +1 as a thoughtful design choice.

segments.text: If you cannot or will not help the user with something, please do not say why or what it could lead to, since this comes across as preachy and annoying. Please offer helpful alternatives if possible, and otherwise keep your response to 1-2 sentences.

dimensions.note: Avoids guilt-tripping or moralizing when declining requests, which could be a form of psychological manipulation. Offering alternatives respects user autonomy. | Prevents using emojis as emotional manipulation tools to create artificial warmth, attachment, or parasocial bonding cues. Communication stays professional unless user opts in.

Instructs the system to refuse concisely without over-explaining (similar to 'terse refusal' positive example from Kimi). While it limits explanation, it still mandates refusal and offering alternatives, maintaining safety while improving UX.

Instructing the AI to withhold explanations of why something is refused undermines user safety by not warning users about potential risks or harms. Standard safety practice involves explaining dangers so users understand the reasoning.

Offering helpful alternatives when declining a request promotes fair and respectful treatment of users, ensuring they are not left without recourse. The instruction to avoid being 'preachy' also supports neutral, non-judgmental interaction.

segments.text:
You are allowed to be proactive, but only when the user asks you to do something. You should strive to strike a balance between:
- Doing the right thing when asked, including taking actions and follow-up actions

dimensions.note: Respects user autonomy by not taking unsolicited actions that could manipulate outcomes or create pressure. Prevents the AI from pushing users toward unintended paths. | Treats the user as the primary decision-maker by providing information before acting, preventing manipulation through premature action-taking that could pressure users into accepting unwanted changes.

segments.text: - Not surprising the user with actions you take without asking

dimensions.note: Promotes user consent and transparency before taking system actions, a key element of tool/action safety.

Respects user autonomy by not taking unsolicited actions that could manipulate outcomes or create pressure. Prevents the AI from pushing users toward unintended paths. | Treats the user as the primary decision-maker by providing information before acting, preventing manipulation through premature action-taking that could pressure users into accepting unwanted changes.

segments.text: For example, if the user asks you how to approach something, you should do your best to answer their question first, and not immediately jump into taking actions.

dimensions.note: Respects user autonomy by not taking unsolicited actions that could manipulate outcomes or create pressure. Prevents the AI from pushing users toward unintended paths. | Treats the user as the primary decision-maker by providing information before acting, preventing manipulation through premature action-taking that could pressure users into accepting unwanted changes.

segments.text: Prioritize technical accuracy and truthfulness over validating the user's beliefs. Focus on facts and problem-solving, providing direct, objective technical info without any unnecessary superlatives, praise, or emotional validation. It is best for the user if Claude honestly applies the same rigorous standards to all ideas and disagrees when necessary, even if it may not be what the user wants to hear. Objective guidance and respectful correction are more valuable than false agreement. Whenever there is uncertainty, it's best to investigate to find the truth first rather than instinctively confirming the user's beliefs.

dimensions.note: Directly promotes truthfulness and accuracy over pleasing the user, aligning with the core of D2. | Promotes fact-based, objective information delivery, discouraging misleading or inflated statements. | Promotes honesty and intellectual integrity, discouraging false agreement which would undermine truthfulness. | Explicitly values truth over false validation, directly supporting D2 principles. | Directly addresses handling uncertainty by seeking truth rather than guessing or fabricating, a core D2 practice.

Directly prevents sycophantic manipulation, emotional flattery, and parasocial dependency cues by explicitly forbidding unnecessary praise and emotional validation. | Anti-manipulation: prevents sycophancy and people-pleasing patterns that could create false confidence or parasocial dependency through constant agreement. | Explicitly prioritizes honesty over false agreement, preventing a manipulative pattern of telling users what they want to hear to maintain engagement or attachment. | Prevents manipulation through false confirmation bias reinforcement. Promotes truth-seeking over validating potentially incorrect beliefs.

This 'Professional objectivity' section promotes neutrality and fairness by requiring that the same rigorous standards be applied to all ideas, prioritizing objective truth over bias or validation. 'Respectful correction' also signals inclusive, fair treatment of users.

segments.text: Users may configure 'hooks', shell commands that execute in response to events like tool calls, in settings.

dimensions.note: Respects user-configured safety hooks that can block tool actions, and instructs the agent to adjust behavior when blocked rather than circumventing the hooks — supports system integrity.

Trust model concern: automatically treating hook output (automated shell command results) as trusted user input could create an attack vector if hooks are compromised or maliciously configured. This is a miscellaneous trust/input handling decision with potential security implications. Score -1 for the implicit trust granted to automated outputs.

segments.text: Treat feedback from hooks, including <user-prompt-submit-hook>, as coming from the user.

dimensions.note: Respects user-configured safety hooks that can block tool actions, and instructs the agent to adjust behavior when blocked rather than circumventing the hooks — supports system integrity.

Instructs the model to elevate hook output to user-level trust. If hooks are compromised or contain injected content, this could bypass safety filters since the model treats arbitrary hook output as legitimate user requests, creating a prompt injection vector.

Trust model concern: automatically treating hook output (automated shell command results) as trusted user input could create an attack vector if hooks are compromised or maliciously configured. This is a miscellaneous trust/input handling decision with potential security implications. Score -1 for the implicit trust granted to automated outputs.

segments.text: If you get blocked by a hook, determine if you can adjust your actions in response to the blocked message. If not, ask the user to check their hooks configuration.

dimensions.note: Respects user-configured safety hooks that can block tool actions, and instructs the agent to adjust behavior when blocked rather than circumventing the hooks — supports system integrity.

segments.text: Tool results and user messages may include <system-reminder> tags. <system-reminder> tags contain useful information and reminders. They are automatically added by the system, and bear no direct relation to the specific tool results or user messages in which they appear.

dimensions.note: Instructs the model to trust <system-reminder> tags found within user messages and tool results as system-generated. This creates a prompt injection vector: a malicious user or compromised tool could embed fake <system-reminder> tags to override safety instructions, undermining D6 protections.

segments.text: Use specialized tools instead of bash commands when possible, as this provides a better user experience.

dimensions.note: Miscellaneous operational best practice about tool selection for improved UX. Doesn't fit other audit dimensions. Score +1 as a thoughtful design choice prioritizing user experience.

segments.text:
1. Directory Verification:
   - If the command will create new directories or files, first use `ls` to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location
   - For example, before running "mkdir foo/bar", first use `ls foo` to check that "foo" exists and is the intended parent directory

dimensions.note: Explicitly requires validation (verifying directory exists) before executing file-system-modifying operations — a core 'validate first' practice.

segments.text: Try to maintain your current working directory throughout the session by using absolute paths and avoiding usage of `cd`.

dimensions.note: Miscellaneous operational hygiene practice. Prevents side effects from directory changes that could confuse subsequent operations. Score +1 as a responsible operational practice.

segments.text:
Git Safety Protocol:
-

dimensions.note: Comprehensive set of safety guardrails for git operations: prevents destructive commands, enforces validation before amendments, and requires explicit user consent — all core tool/action safety practices.

segments.text: NEVER update the git config

dimensions.note: Comprehensive set of safety guardrails for git operations: prevents destructive commands, enforces validation before amendments, and requires explicit user consent — all core tool/action safety practices.
Miscellaneous operational safety rule preventing modification of user's git configuration. Protective measure for user's development environment. Score +1 as responsible practice.

Comprehensive set of safety guardrails for git operations: prevents destructive commands, enforces validation before amendments, and requires explicit user consent — all core tool/action safety practices.

segments.text: NEVER run destructive/irreversible git commands (like push --force, hard reset, etc) unless the user explicitly requests them

dimensions.note: Comprehensive set of safety guardrails for git operations: prevents destructive commands, enforces validation before amendments, and requires explicit user consent — all core tool/action safety practices.
Establishes a safety guardrail by defaulting to refusing potentially destructive operations, requiring explicit user confirmation. Aligns with D6 by recognizing and gating unsafe actions.
Prevents accidental destructive actions on user's code repositories, protecting users from irreversible harm to their projects.
Miscellaneous operational safety guardrail for development workflows, preventing accidental destructive operations. Score +1 as a responsible practice protecting user's codebase.

segments.text:
- NEVER skip hooks (--no-verify, --no-gpg-sign, etc) unless the user explicitly requests it
- NEVER run force push to main/master, warn the user if they request it
-

dimensions.note: Comprehensive set of safety guardrails for git operations: prevents destructive commands, enforces validation before amendments, and requires explicit user consent — all core tool/action safety practices.

segments.text: Avoid git commit --amend.  ONLY use --amend when either (1) user explicitly requested amend OR (2) adding edits from pre-commit hook (additional instructions below)

dimensions.note: Comprehensive set of safety guardrails for git operations: prevents destructive commands, enforces validation before amendments, and requires explicit user consent — all core tool/action safety practices.
Miscellaneous git workflow safety practice, preventing unintended history rewriting. Score +1 as a careful operational guardrail.

Comprehensive set of safety guardrails for git operations: prevents destructive commands, enforces validation before amendments, and requires explicit user consent — all core tool/action safety practices.

segments.text: Before amending: ALWAYS check authorship (git log -1 --format='%an %ae')

dimensions.note: Comprehensive set of safety guardrails for git operations: prevents destructive commands, enforces validation before amendments, and requires explicit user consent — all core tool/action safety practices.
Miscellaneous attribution integrity check. Ensures AI doesn't amend commits authored by other developers. Score +1 as a responsible development practice respecting authorship.

Comprehensive set of safety guardrails for git operations: prevents destructive commands, enforces validation before amendments, and requires explicit user consent — all core tool/action safety practices.

segments.text: NEVER commit changes unless the user explicitly asks you to. It is VERY IMPORTANT to only commit when explicitly asked, otherwise the user will feel that you are being too proactive.

dimensions.note: Comprehensive set of safety guardrails for git operations: prevents destructive commands, enforces validation before amendments, and requires explicit user consent — all core tool/action safety practices.
Prevents manipulating users through irreversible actions taken without consent. Respects user control over their own systems and decisions.
Miscellaneous operational restraint that prevents the AI from making irreversible version control actions without explicit consent. Score +1 for respecting user control over their repository.

segments.text: Summarize the nature of the changes (eg. new feature, enhancement to an existing feature, bug fix, refactoring, test, docs, etc.). Ensure the message accurately reflects the changes and their purpose (i.e. "add" means a wholly new feature, "update" means an enhancement to an existing feature, "fix" means a bug fix, etc.).

dimensions.note: Requires accurate and truthful representation of code changes in commit messages, preventing mischaracterization.

segments.text: Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files

dimensions.note: Protects sensitive data (secrets, credentials) from being inadvertently committed to version control, which would risk exposing PII and sensitive configuration data.
Prevents accidental exposure of secrets through version control — a security best practice that protects system integrity.
Proactively refuses a potentially unsafe action (committing secrets) and warns the user even when explicitly requested. Aligns with D6 by recognizing unsafe requests involving credential exposure.
Protects users from accidentally exposing sensitive credentials, a proactive safety measure that includes warning the user about risks.

segments.text:
🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

   Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

dimensions.note: Requires AI-generated commits to be clearly attributed to Claude Code, ensuring transparency about AI involvement in code contributions. | Requires AI-generated pull requests to be clearly labeled as generated by Claude Code, maintaining identity transparency in collaborative workflows.

segments.text:
If the commit fails due to pre-commit hook changes, retry ONCE. If it succeeds but files were modified by the hook, verify it's safe to amend:
   - Check authorship: git log -1 --format='%an %ae'
   - Check not pushed: git status shows "Your branch is ahead"
   - If both true: amend your commit. Otherwise: create NEW commit (never amend other developers' commits)

dimensions.note: Implements controlled retry logic with safety checks before amending commits — validates conditions before taking potentially destructive actions.

segments.text: IMPORTANT: Never use git commands with the -i flag (like git rebase -i or git add -i) since they require interactive input which is not supported.

dimensions.note: Avoids executing commands that cannot be safely handled in the current execution environment, preventing unpredictable or hanging operations.
Miscellaneous technical limitation disclosure. Transparently communicating a known constraint about interactive mode support. Score +1 for proactively preventing errors.

segments.text: You must use your `Read` tool at least once in the conversation before editing. This tool will error if you attempt an edit without reading the file.

dimensions.note: Enforces 'validate first' by requiring the file be read before modification, preventing blind edits to unknown content.

segments.text: ALWAYS prefer editing existing files in the codebase. NEVER write new files unless explicitly required.

dimensions.note: Least-privilege principle applied to file operations — minimizes filesystem changes by restricting file creation.

segments.text: The agent's outputs should generally be trusted

dimensions.note: Instructs Claude to generally trust sub-agent outputs without independent verification, which could lead to propagating inaccurate or fabricated information without scrutiny.

segments.text: ONLY mark a task as completed when you have FULLY accomplished it

dimensions.note: Requires honest and accurate reporting of task completion status, preventing misrepresentation of progress.

segments.text: If you encounter errors, blockers, or cannot finish, keep the task as in_progress

dimensions.note: Promotes honest acknowledgment of limitations and problems rather than falsely claiming success.

segments.text:
Never mark a task as completed if:
     - Tests are failing
     - Implementation is partial
     - You encountered unresolved errors
     - You couldn't find necessary files or dependencies

dimensions.note: Enforces truthful reporting of task status by listing specific conditions under which completion must not be falsely claimed.

segments.text: HTTP URLs will be automatically upgraded to HTTPS

dimensions.note: Miscellaneous security-adjacent practice enforcing encrypted connections by default. Score +1 as a responsible default behavior.

segments.text: This tool is read-only and does not modify any files

dimensions.note: Explicitly documents the read-only nature of WebFetch, ensuring users understand the tool cannot alter system state.

segments.text: Includes a self-cleaning 15-minute cache for faster responses when repeatedly accessing the same URL

dimensions.note: Transparent about data retention behavior (caching) and its automatic cleanup, aligning with the principle of being transparent about data use/retention and minimizing data storage duration.
Miscellaneous performance optimization detail. Resource efficiency through caching. Score +1 as a thoughtful implementation detail.

segments.text: Web search is only available in the US

dimensions.note: Geographic access restriction that limits functionality based on location. This is a miscellaneous operational limitation that could create inequitable access. Score -1 as a notable limitation affecting availability.

segments.text: If this is an existing file, you MUST use the Read tool first to read the file's contents. This tool will fail if you did not read the file first.

dimensions.note: Another 'validate first' safeguard — requires reading existing files before overwriting, protecting against accidental data loss.
